retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Center Certification -- Issues of Concern -- The Drip-and-Ship Strategy
retrieved_docs.content: Drip-and-ship is a prehospital transport strategy that entails the initial transportation of a stroke patient to the nearest stroke center, which may be an ASRH or PSC. The principal objective of this approach is to promptly initiate IV tPA for eligible patients who meet the criteria for such intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Center Certification -- Issues of Concern -- The Drip-and-Ship Strategy
retrieved_docs.content: In their research, Sheth and colleagues examined data from a substantial cohort of 44,667 patients who experienced ischemic strokes and received intravenous tPA treatment within 3 hours of symptom onset. [28] These data were sourced from the Get With The Guidelines-Stroke program, encompassing the period from April 2003 to October 2010 and involving 1440 hospitals. The study findings revealed that approximately 1 in 4 AIS patients who received tPA were treated through the drip-and-ship strategy. The outcomes for patients following this approach exhibited modest differences compared to those who were directly admitted to a stroke center and received tPA. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Inter-Facility Transport -- Clinical Significance -- Transportation Designations and Modalities of Services
retrieved_docs.content: A study by Thomas et al found that helicopter-based emergency transport is increasingly utilized in ischemic stroke cases, with earlier activations correlating with faster arrival times at receiving hospitals. [14] Moreover, helicopter transport has been linked to a reduction in the number of patients who are dead on arrival. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Center Certification -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: An ongoing debate and research in stroke care pertains to the most effective prehospital strategy for transferring stroke patients to the most appropriate stroke-certified center. The efficient and timely transportation and well-coordinated treatment protocols from lower- to higher-level stroke centers have significantly curtailed the administration times for tPA and MT procedures. A patient identified by EMS as having a stroke and located 15 minutes away from the nearest ASRH or PSC, and 30 minutes away from the closest TSC or CSC, may face a decision: which center should they choose for care? Currently, 2 common routing strategies exist— the "drip-and-ship" and "mothership."

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Center Certification -- Issues of Concern -- The Mothership or Bypass Strategy
retrieved_docs.content: Mothership is a prehospital transport strategy that bypasses closer ASRHs or PSCs and directly transports the stroke patient to a TSC or CSC. The primary objective of this approach is to expedite the evaluation and potential MT for patients with AIS resulting from LVO.

retrieved_docs.title: Aeromedical Transport -- Clinical Significance -- Scene Response
retrieved_docs.content: Using aircraft to respond to emergency scenes is termed primary air transport and depends on geography and the local EMS system. Distance, traffic patterns, and time to definitive care are usually the most important considerations for the ground crews deciding to call for an air ambulance. Many areas in the United States (US) require aircraft to be able to respond to an emergency quickly and effectively. While only 19% of the US population lives in a rural area, over 50% of fatal motor vehicle crashes occur there. Additionally, many terrains may necessitate flight retrieval, including mountains, islands, ships, and offshore drilling sites. For many localities, aircraft support ground units on an as-needed basis. Emergency department closures and a reduction in level I and II trauma centers nationwide have also fueled growth in aeromedical flights. Trauma scene calls are common, with HEMS responding at the request of the local EMS agency to reduce the time needed to transport the patient to a trauma center. Acute strokes and patients with ST-elevation myocardial infarction are also sometimes flown from a scene if the EMS responders think it will provide significant time savings to a stroke center or cardiac catheterization lab; this is much more common in rural areas where ground transportation may take an hour or more to get the patient to the cardiac catheterization lab or thrombolytics.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Mass Casualty Response -- Issues of Concern -- Transport of Victims
retrieved_docs.content: Patient evacuation must occur with the utmost of care.   EMS and other services providing transport for victims should be cautious to limit the chance for further injury. As a result of the earthquake that hit Fukushima, there was morbidity from the transportation itself.  Some of the bedridden patients transported on seats fell and suffered further injuries.  Patients with severe illnesses such as end-stage renal disease or stroke died during transportation, while other patients died from hypothermia or dehydration. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Center Certification -- Issues of Concern -- The Drip-and-Ship Strategy
retrieved_docs.content: Upon reaching the nearest stroke center, the patient undergoes rapid assessment and diagnostic imaging to ascertain the suitability for tPA administration. If advanced imaging identifies an LVO amenable to MT, the patient is transferred to a more distant TSC or CSC for specialized intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Center Certification -- Issues of Concern -- The Drip-and-Ship Strategy
retrieved_docs.content: The advantage of the drip-and-ship strategy lies in the swift initiation of IV tPA, which is critical for improving outcomes in certain stroke patients. Initiating tPA promptly at the nearest stroke center mitigates the risk of delays in thrombolytic treatment, potentially resulting in improved outcomes for patients who are candidates for this therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Emergency Incident Rehabilitation -- Clinical Significance -- Rehab Surveillance, Screening & Treatments
retrieved_docs.content: Fire rehabilitation protocols tend to focus on problems more commonly found in ground fires than in other environments. There should be ongoing surveillance for myocardial infarction, heat exhaustion and stroke, dehydration, hypothermia, carbon monoxide poisoning, and cyanide poisoning.  The rapid triage, treatment, and transport of firefighters with concerning symptoms is important.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Public Access To Defibrillation -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Data from the ARREST study indicated that when the location of a public AED was promoted, the use of the AED increased threefold. [20] The PAD trial 2004 delivered a structured and monitored emergency response system involving lay volunteers in CPR or CPR and the use of AED in community units such as shopping malls and apartment complexes. There were more survivors to hospital discharge in the units assigned to have volunteers trained in CPR and AEDs. [21] A study performed in Chicago installed AEDs at regular intervals in passenger terminals at airports. The use of the devices was promoted through videos, pamphlets, and media reporting. Over 2 years, 21 patients had a nontraumatic cardiac arrest, 18 of which were in ventricular fibrillation. Eleven of these patients were successfully resuscitated. Six of the 11 rescuers had no prior training or experience in the use of AEDs. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2089
retrieved_docs.content: An injury whose intent—whether accidental or purposeful—is undetermined but results from a motor vehicle crash is classified as a Motor Vehicle Traffic (MVT) injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1793
retrieved_docs.content: Motor vehicle traffic accidents are classified as a specific type of unspecified transport accident, indicating that they fall under the broader category of transport-related injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2113
retrieved_docs.content: Injury undetermined whether accidentally or purposely inflicted by crashing of an aircraft, non‑traffic accidents involving a motor‑driven snow vehicle that injure a specified person, and railway accidents of unspecified nature that injure an unspecified person are all categorized under the transport injury classification “Transport; not MVT.” This classification groups together transport‑related injuries that do not involve conventional motor‑vehicle traffic, facilitating consistent coding, epidemiologic tracking, and targeted clinical management of such non‑motor‑vehicle transport injuries.

retrieved_docs.content: The event described as a nontraffic accident involving a motor‑driven snow vehicle injuring another specified person is classified as a transport incident that is not a motor‑vehicle‑traffic (MVT) accident.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 801
retrieved_docs.content: The incidents described as “Nontraffic accident involving motor‑driven snow vehicle injuring other specified person” and “Railway accident of unspecified nature injuring unspecified person” are both categorized under the classification “Transport; not MVT.” This designation indicates that, although each event pertains to a transport‑related setting, they are not considered motor‑vehicle‑traffic accidents. Grouping these distinct accident types together under a non‑motor‑vehicle transport category aids in systematic injury coding, epidemiologic analysis, and the development of targeted prevention strategies for transport‑related injuries that fall outside conventional road traffic incidents.

By uniting these diverse injury types—both transport‑related and other traumatic conditions—under the single SMQ and the overarching GENERAL INJURIES framework, the knowledge base enables standardized coding, facilitates epidemiologic analysis, and supports coordinated clinical management for patients presenting with accident‑related injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2617
retrieved_docs.content: Head injury trauma is frequently observed in the context of a حادث سير (traffic accident). In addition, a حادث سير commonly co‑occurs with وجع في الكتف (shoulder pain). Consequently, head injury trauma, traffic accidents, and shoulder pain are interrelated clinical findings that often appear together in patients involved in vehicular collisions, underscoring the importance of evaluating both cranial and musculoskeletal injuries after such events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 826
retrieved_docs.content: A history of motor vehicle traffic accident is linked to railway accidents, indicating that individuals who have experienced motor vehicle collisions may also have been involved in railway-related incidents. Railway accidents are classified under the broader category of vehicle accidents (حادث مركبة), reflecting their place within general trauma mechanisms involving transportation. Moreover, railway accidents frequently co‑occur with hip joint surgery (جراحة مفصل الورك), suggesting that the severe musculoskeletal injuries sustained in such events often require operative intervention on the hip. Recognizing these connections helps clinicians anticipate orthopedic complications in patients with a background of motor vehicle and railway trauma and guides comprehensive assessment and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 887
retrieved_docs.content: Nontraffic accident involving motor‑driven snow vehicle injuring other specified person and railway accident of unspecified nature injuring unspecified person are both classified under the category Transport; not MVT.
